query: Routine AI complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: BBB study finds most AI-related customer reviews are negative | Local News | ktvo.com
results.content:
The organization also reported receiving nearly 100,000 AI and automation-related complaints, reviews and scam reports over the past three years.

The study found many consumers felt confused, ignored or unable to resolve issues when interacting with automated systems.

Complaints were especially common in online retail, e-commerce and technology-focused industries.

> "People's main complaint is that they want to have an opportunity during the customer service experience or transaction to talk to a human, and I think what's most frustrating for them is when that doesn't happen," said Kesha Duncan, regional director for the Better Business Bureau. [...] BBB officials said consumers frequently expressed dissatisfaction with businesses that relied heavily on automation without providing a clear path to a human representative.

Many reported difficulties resolving problems, escalating complaints or receiving personalized assistance.

The report found AI-related complaints and reviews have increased steadily in recent years.

The BBB recorded more than 22,000 complaints mentioning AI or automation in 2023, more than 25,000 in 2024 and nearly 34,000 in 2025.

While the organization acknowledged that AI can help businesses improve efficiency, it recommends companies maintain opportunities for customers to speak with a person when needed. [...] BBB study finds most AI-related customer reviews are negative

A new Better Business Bureau study suggests many consumers are growing frustrated with the increasing use of artificial intelligence in customer service, particularly when it becomes difficult to reach a real person for help. (File)

COLUMBIA, Mo. - A new Better Business Bureau study suggests many consumers are growing frustrated with the increasing use of artificial intelligence in customer service, particularly when it becomes difficult to reach a real person for help.

According to the BBB's International Investigations Initiative, more than 90% of nearly 20,000 business reviews mentioning AI since 2023 expressed negative sentiment.

results.title: Routine - Plugged In
results.content:
## CONTENT CONCERNS

Some will find the short, unguided play of Routine to be an interesting and immersive challenge. Others will find the game very frustrating.

The game is also designed to instill fear in players. The atmosphere is intensely creepy at times. And a large part of play is made up of running to find some sort of shelter while you hear foes closing in from behind in the deathly quiet environs. The enemies are not typical. They listen and search. They react to light and track you down.

It’s not difficult to find cover if you stay aware of your surroundings. But if you’re caught, enemies will crush and stab you. And the game ends in your death. [...] After your initial landing, however, Routine doesn’t give you much help at all.There are no arrows, blinking lights, splashes of paint or objective menus to point out the proper path. And there’s very little to indicate what you’re even there to do. If you look closely enough and experiment with your CAT, you’ll find some emails stating that the base was rocked by a lunar quake, and some suspicious messages indicate that the company in charge was working to cover it up.

If you want to know more, you’ll need to scour the disquietingly shadowed hallways, crawl spaces, deteriorating living quarters, and computer terminals in a very quiet, broken-down and extremely unpopulated moon plaza.

You walk silently, search in detail, and listen carefully.

results.title: Frustrated by AI applications
results.content: - So ignoring the broader topic of A.I. I think your specific comments about A.I. and recruiting, its a natural response for applicants to start using A.I. and automation to automate their applications as firms use A.I. and automation to automate filters. Yes, on the one hand its frustrating, but on the other, from the applicants standpoint that is what they need to do in order to even get their resume in front of a hiring manager's eyes, because if you are using these systems the wrong phrasing will score your resume below other candidates and ensure that you are filtered before any human ever even looks at your resume. I get the frustration - I think the real answer is to change how we do recruitment... but beyond that I don't really have good solutions... the reality is that [...] - When we added typewriters, people said we would forget how to write. We added calculators and they said we wouldn’t be able to do math. (Math got more advanced) We now have Ai and people are worried we will not be able to formulate thoughts. Yet you have to practice and get good at putting clear and correct info into Ai. You also have to critically think about the answers that come out and make sure they aren’t junk. I have no problem or concerns with Ai and this generation. The young people we hire are different. But we all were. The one that concerns me a little is the driving skills and distraction. [...] I don't really have good solutions... the reality is that recruitment as a whole has been very broken for at least a decade now, its just that A.I. has become accessable enough that a lot of candidates now know how to game the screening process and so hiring managers can't ignore the problem.

results.title: ROUTINE - Before You Buy
results.content: most unsettling stuff in routine are the parts where you're alone with just you and the suffocating darkness of the station, aware that you're cut off from any kind of help or rescue. That's that's more scary. The anticipation of something scary is much more effective than the actual horror stuff. And I don't think any of that is outright bad. I really don't. In fact, nothing in this game is outright bad. It's It's mostly fine to good. Like, this could have been a really frustrating game to get through and it actually wasn't. If anything, it's maybe a little bit too accommodating and easy, but I'd rather ultimately have that than frustrated and miserable. For all the faults I did list, this is not a chore to play it. It it there are a couple of boring parts and a couple of tedious [...] audio logs, but the voice acting is really well done. The writing is straightforward, but with lots of personality. I appreciate they mix things up from time to time, too, like finding worksheet printouts explaining how your cat works and whiteboards with sticky notes explaining the timeline of events. The plot at first seems like your average Hell 9,000 evil computer run them up sort of thing, but without spoilers, I will say there is a lot more to it. This game goes in some weird and intriguing directions with the whole thing, and it gets a little trippier by the end. Um, I do wish that it was a little longer. That's my main complaint there, cuz the story is quality. Could have lived with another hour or two. You could finish this thing in a lazy afternoon if you want. There's not a lot [...] keep track of is the battery life on this thing. It's got three discharges before it needs a recharge, but for the most part, batteries are pretty plentiful, so it's not really that much of a concern. And I know I sound like I'm super positive on everything. And I am. I mean, I'm all right. For as much as I love the art design and everything from the settings to the atmospheric to the soundsscape, which is unsettling appropriately so, the gameplay of Routine is I mean depending on your perspective uh could be fortunately, it could be unfortunately, but it is a little routine. There's no escaping that. It's it's not bad at all, but for the game short length, you're not really doing that much, which is fine for the parts that are just like pure atmosphere, especially the beginning. But once

results.title: 15 AI Chatbot Problems in Customer Support — and How to Fix Each One
results.content:
Chatbot problems surfacing as complaints rather than logged errors stem from content and experience design failures rather than technical malfunctions in the AI platform. Questions the knowledge base doesn't cover at all, situations where responses are technically correct but practically unhelpful for the customer's actual context, and escalation friction forcing unnecessary loops before reaching a human agent all generate dissatisfaction invisible to standard monitoring. These failures produce no error logs or system alerts but consistently drive the customer frustration that appears in satisfaction surveys, social media complaints, and the support tickets mentioning the chatbot negatively. [...] Fixing a complaint-generating chatbot typically takes two to four weeks when the root cause involves knowledge quality rather than model limitations. The timeline breaks into one week identifying the top complaint patterns and tracing them to specific content gaps or structural problems, one to two weeks restructuring the affected content with proper metadata and scope boundaries, and several days of validation testing confirming measurable improvement before redeploying. Companies that adjust only AI settings without fixing underlying content see no meaningful or sustained improvement. [...] Back to Blog →

Frequently asked questions

### Companies deploy AI chatbots expecting to reduce costs but end up frustrating customers and increasing escalations. What causes support chatbots to fail even as underlying AI technology improves?
